speaker: interviewer
text: The interviewer, Deborah Cutler, Los Angeles, English. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about Agde.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we came to this camp Agde, and it turned out to be a rotten place. I mean, there were barracks, empty barracks. We came there with children, women, and families-- you know, whole families-- and once they put us in, there was a gate with-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what do you call this-- barbed wire, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] empty barracks. There were some soldiers. They came, OK, you get ready, get some straw and make your-- make your beds to, to sleep for tonight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the, the men and the boys-- like me, I went out with the, the men to get-- there was a whole mountain like of straw, straw bundled, you know, for-- like on the fields. We took this. We took it in. They gave us like a sack. We filled the sack with straw, and this was our mattress. And the white sheets were our shirts. I mean, they were just trying to tell us that we shouldn't revolt or something to get us into this camp. So once we were in there, we had to do the best we could. The, the women and children prepared, prepared the, the, the straw bags for the night. And when, when the straw mattress were finished, the authorities-- this was a camp with-- there were soldiers already, and police, gendarmerie. They came into the barracks, says OK. All the men line up. They took out the men, and they put them in another camp-- I mean, in a different area of barracks, separated also by barbed wire, where women and men could not see each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the middle. I could stay with my mother, or I could go with my dad. So I stayed with my mother. And for me, it was all an adventure. You know, we were kids there. We had barracks, we were sleeping on straw. It was camping for me. Because we did not realize-- we did not realize really the troubles we were in. Our parents knew. But you know, children play. We, we had-- our neighbors were prisoners of war from-- it was called Indochina. It was-- Vietnam today, I think, where the French were fighting. And there were thousands of Indonesia-- Indochina prisoners next to those women and children. You know, you see this, you get frightened. But they were very gentle people. They were nice people. They were prisoners. They tried to help. They were behind barbed wire too, separated, of course, from us. But we kids, we were playing there. We were playing soldiers. There were military helmets from military equipment. We took bats, we took helmets, we threw rocks, and we played. It was a playground for us. Until one day, they decided I'm too big to be with the women and the children. They took me over to my dad, where the men are. In the meantime, like I said, we were there. It was September-- September about-- we were in Agde-- and September, October. My brother, who, like I said before, was very fragile. He was sick. He got sick again, and he had to be taken to the hospital. So there was a city near Agde called Beziers. It was about 20 kilometers from Agde camp. So they took my brother to this hospital. And my mother got permission to visit. I mean, this was not a concentration camp. We were considered families and people, and not runaways or things. In the beginning it was OK. So my mother could visit. And then later on, I got sick. I got high fever and terrible headaches. And they were afraid maybe I got typhoid or something. They brought me into the hospital, too. So here I was in the same hospital. My brother was on the second floor. I was on the third floor. My parents were in the camp. My father separate, and my mother and sister separate. I don't know how they did it. They got permission to visit us once, on a Sunday. They got the permit. They could take the train to come to the hospital, and they had to be back by 7 o'clock in the evening. They came at noon. They visited my brother. They took him out from bed. He was feeling better. I mean, this was-- I was already also a week in the hospital. I was ready to be discharged in a couple days or so. My parents came on a Sunday. They told us get dressed. We had-- we had our clothes nearby. It was in a hospital. We were laying in a room with another 20 people. It was not to a hospital where you lay by yourself in a room. You had 20 people. And to make this story short, they made us walk out from the hospital. We walked to the train station, and we went back to Luchon, to the village where we came from, not knowing where we are going, what will expect us there. We just left for there. And my mother and father decided that me and my father should go one station before Luchon so that we are not coming as a family together. Because as a small village, you see, come maybe five, six people, ten people, on the train station. And we don't look like French peasants there. So-- and it was more-- later on, it was much, much, more obvious that I walked with my dad. It was about five kilometers, was a station before Luchon. So I went out with my father, and my mother-- and sister and mother continued with the train to the end. Now, my dad and I was walking on a highway. It was the route nationale in France, going into Luchon. And we were both walking, and we didn't look like peasants. My father with a suitcase, and I have a little bundle under my hand. And two gendarmes on bicycles passed us. Usually, you saw a gendarme, you were shivering already, because this-- they were terrible. The gendarmerie in France was-- I could say as, as bad as the Germans. They, they helped a lot to, to, to, to make the misery bigger than it was. Anyway, we walked. My father said later, he said they must've gotten blind. An angel must've been with us. They drove by. They didn't ask us for paper, because France was known-- when you see somebody in the street-- the authority-- let me see your papers. Etranger means refugee. They take you. It doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were lucky. We made it to the village. Where do we go? To the apartment where we lived before, to this printer. This printer saw us, he cried, he was so happy to see us. We explained what happened, that we come from a camp. He said don't worry. My son will take you in his house. It was six kilometers from there, in the village. There was no Jews. There was just-- there were maybe 400 peasants-- I mean, people-- not family-- 400 people. And he sent for his son. The son came, and the son said, don't worry, I take you to my-- he did not-- this man must have know what he went into, because the Germans made sure always to announce, if you keep Jews in your house, you will be shot. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they took us into this village. It was one building with a big living room, where there was a chimney, where we cooked in and we warmed ourself in. And we lived together with this family. This was a man, his wife, and two children. They kept us in this build-- in this house, and to make myself-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] to help, I ask if there is peasant who need people to help him on the fields to work. I found a family who gave me work. They-- the-- we worked on the fields. It was-- it was winter. It was January of '41, when we came back to this place, Luchon, and we went to Juzet-de-Luchon. It was January. There was not much field work to do, but there were cows to be milked, and to-- the stables have to be cleaned out. Wood has to be sawn, because there was no gas. There was no-- the heating was done by a chimney, by chimney. And like I said, we were sitting in the winter in the evening. There was no radio. There was nothing. We are sitting in front of the chimney with this French family. We spoke already a little broken French, my sister, and my brother, and I. And we could, we could manage to talk. We were sitting with our parents, with them warming, and cooking, and living together. There was three rooms total. We five were in one room. And after a while, later on, this man, whose name was Clement, built a little kitchen in the back, so that we can use it. We couldn't even go through the house to the kitchen. We have to go outside. And there was a chicken coop. He made from the chicken coop-- he built us a kitchen. The people were very nice. And his boy was my age. And his daughter was the age of my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lived a peasant life there. I worked with the farmers. It became slowly spring, and people started working on the fields. I worked on the fields [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sowing, you say-- putting potatoes in, taking the cows out in the pasture. And we went up to the mountains, in the woods to cut trees- prepare trees for the next winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: The trees, when they fell them, they have to dry. They cannot burn in the same year. So trees were felled. And I made myself available to all kind of work. And for this, they gave me potatoes. They gave us milk. They made butter. And it was very, very convenient for us, and we could survive. In the summer, when the fruit came out, this, this, his-- this son of his was-- his name is Jano-- he showed me all the tricks of the trades, like to say how to ride horses. In the winter, he showed me how to ski, because when it was snowing, we couldn't use the highways. And to get food from one house to another, we were skiing from one house-- like cross country. And I learned skiing. When you're young-- I felt like I'm in a-- what do you call it-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on vacation. But I worked hard, and I liked it. I always was strong. I ate good. I drank good. And maybe this helped me for the future. This is what I do in the future to survive. But everything is not-- everything doesn't turn out forever. It came-- the mayor of this little village knew that we are a Jewish family living there. And he started to pressure the family where we lived, as he's afraid that if it comes out, that they find out that they're keeping a Jewish family in this village, that they will-- they will get in trouble. And as mayor, he, he, he wants to, to, to take care that his village will not be damaged.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what is there to do? It was 1941. We were the whole year almost there. And where do you go from here? I mean you don't go to the authorities and say, we are Jews. We lived in the village, take us. We are not ready to turn belly up. So the mayor said, well, I can give you a paper where you will be sent for résidence forcée. This means forced resident. We send you to another village. And you stay there in this village, and see what happens. There are more of your people there. So we didn't have much choice, because he says, if you don't do this, then he has to go to the authority in Luchon and tell them that there's a Jewish family living in this village. I mean, the French were good to a certain point. When it came to their own skin, some, some of them, they were nasty.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave us a paper, and our family, we got ready. I mean, it was heartbreaking, because we grew together to this family, and they loved us. And we'd liked them. And we had to leave. There came the time we had to leave, and we left for another place called Aulus-les-Bains. It is not too far from there, but it is close to the border-- it was also the Pyrenees and close to the border of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Andorra. Very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, we went to the city. Came up by bus. And there was already a Jewish community. There was a little committee there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, we again-- my father always said when we're with a bunch of ours together, this is problems, because it is notified by others, and this will be a problem. Anyway, we, we got to this village, and we found a little apartment there. And I started working. First, there was nothing to do. There was-- I don't know how you say it-- you know, in the mountains you have where you break stones for cement. It's like terraces. You work on the mountains. You work with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Quarry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quarry. I worked in a quarry there. I was 15 years old I worked in a quarry for a while, and it was very hard work. And I couldn't continue. And my father took me out. And I tried again on the fields. And we got friendly with the owners of the apartments who rented us. It was a small building. We got together some eggs. We put a chicken on it. And we got little chickies. And we got the little piece of land from the owner of our-- landlord-- so to say. And I planted some [INAUDIBLE], and potatoes, and radishes, and started like a peasant, what I learned from the previous village, what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it started to grow. And we were doing our living there, until one night, in the middle of the night, some people came to the door, knocked on the door. And they shouted, 'Herman, Herman.' These were friends of my father-- 'Herman, the Germans are coming.' 'Oh my God, the Germans are coming.' What do you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He woke us all up. It was midnight. He woke us up. Says, get dressed. Quick, get dressed. So we all got quick dressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do we do? We have to go in the mountains. The Germans are coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew the mountains because I was working around there. So I took them up, about half hour, 45 minutes up, into the mountains. And we sat there the whole-- the rest of the night with, with covers. And it was already cool. And we were sitting there the whole day. And the next day, my father said to me, why don't you go see what's happening, if there's any Germans in the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I slowly got down at day time. I was already dressed like the peasants. I had my beret and with the cane, you know. I was really like a peasant. And came down, and there was nothing. The rest of the, the, the, the people were in the markets. They were talking. Nothing happened. It was a false alarm. So I quick went up and says, there's nothing happens. So we all came back down to the apartment. We came back to the apartment and the day passed. We went to bed. Midnight, again, they tap on the door-- 'Herman. This time they're going to come.' And my dad, he didn't fool around. He got up. Woke us up. My sister says, 'No, I'm not going.' And my mother said, 'What do you-- you saw nothing happened. This is nothing.'

speaker: interviewee
text: He says 'No, we are going.' So we didn't have a choice. Papa is papa in the family. And we went again up the mountains. And we stayed, and the next morning, I, I came down, and I saw a peasant coming up the mountain. He says, 'Oh, you're here.' He says, 'Hide, the Germans took all the Jews from the village.' So then, we started climbing. I mean we went up two hours, three hours, up in the mountains. OK, we, we were up there for about six weeks. I went down nights and grabbed out with my hand the food from the-- what we planted, and made us survive. After six weeks, we could-- we were almost starving. A patrol, a French patrol of gendarmes caught us, took us down to the village, and took us to the train, and brought us to a camp that was called Vernet. Vernet-- my parents, we were all together in Vernet. And it was terrible. Over there, we saw already a concentration of a lot of Jews. From Vernet, they took us to Gurs. Gurs was a camp where they were deporting to Poland. I don't know until today, my, my, my parents and we were together. And we children, my brother and I, we were taken out from the camp to a-- to a-- to a farm, to a boys farm, my sister to a girls' farm. And my father escaped from the camp. My mother was only one left in Gurs. And we were at the boys farm for two months, and got a note from my dad. He was-- in the meantime, he went to Italy-- to Nice, the south of France, because the Italian occupied this part of France. And it was known that the Italian did not let deport their subjects.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father went to Nice, and wrote us a letter-- my-- to my brother and me-- sent us some money for train. Money we were [INAUDIBLE] in this farm school. Sent us money to get to, to Nice to be with the Italian. We went to Nice. And then my sister came, too. We were all four together in Nice. And we were put by the Italian authorities to Aulus-les-Bains to Saint-Martin-Vesubie, what is a village near-- in the Alps near the Italian border. It was occupied by the Italians. We had to present ourself every day twice. Everywhere, family-- about 400 there.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1943, in August, of '43 Badolgio capitulated to the American army in Italy. And Italy broke down. And the Italian army left Nice, and left France for Italy. And they said to us, save your self. You can go wherever you want from this village. We crossed the mountains-- 800 Jews, women, men, and children-- we crossed the mountains over to, to Italy. We were on the way for three days. And we came to the first Italian village, [INAUDIBLE] called. The German were there already. We went down, and when we saw the command car, a German car in the middle of town, my fam-- my brother, sister and father, we went back to the mountains. We hid there. And the Germans took all they can take in this village who came. They knew already the Jews are coming from France. They took them in a camp out of the city. And we stayed in the mountains. We, it was time where you can-- where you have [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know how you say-- it's a fruit off a tree-- we lived from this. We went to some peasants. Some helped us, some didn't, until we got on our way. My father decided-- my sister decided to leave to see if she can get to Rome. She left us in the mountains and she went to the train. And this was it, as far as I knew then. My father, my brother, and I, we took a train two weeks later. We never heard from her. We took the train later, and got from there to Genoa, where we went to a convent. And a priest helped us to-- we tried to get close to the border where the fights-- where the American are liberating-- gave us some money to go to Florence. We arrived in Florence. We went there to a [NON-ENGLISH], to a convent. And they helped us to hide in an old movie theater what was empty. It was an old-- there was a stage, and the benches were taken out on the balcony. And they made room for another-- we were 30 people in this movie house, hidden-- men. The women were taken in convents for women. And there was also a temple in Florence, where I visited. And I met a family, an Italian family with a girl my age what I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fell in love like. You know, I liked her very much. And we went-- and we were friends. And we were in this movie house until one morning-- November 4, I think it was-- at 5 o'clock in the morning, Raus, auf raus, you know, the German voices-- broken in this movie house, broke the doors with, with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rifle butts, moved in, and turned over the beds. Raus, raus. And there were lorries outside already, trucks. And they put us on trucks. At this same moment, there was a little chaos, and my brother, luckily, went up the stairs where the balcony was-- where there was-- all the chairs were stored. And he went under the, the chairs, and he was never seen. They did not see him. So he was safe. We were taken on the-- on the-- to the trucks, with my friend, [? Ludie ?] Goldman, who was a good friend of us. He went with us to Agde. We met back in Italy together, in, in Saint-Martin-Vesubie. And we walked into Italy together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he and his father, and my father, who were friends from Germany, we were all together caught. And they took us to Florence in a big [NON-ENGLISH, a big [NON-ENGLISH], military [NON-ENGLISH]-- and put us in the stables where the horses used to be. And this was in the morning. And during the whole day, they brought in people. They prepared a transport. They brought people, and people, and people. And we went out to the bathroom during the day. And I saw near the bathroom, there was a big-- there was about a 16-foot wall, but had hooks in it, in the wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to my friend this is a place where maybe tonight we can try to escape. And he saw it, too. And we decided to try it tonight. And I told my dad and my dad says, I go with you. And I says OK. We went all three to the toilet. And it was already starting to get dark. My friend, [? Ludie, ?] the first one, he went up one, two, three. And he was over. There was a big barbed wire on top. I don't know how he made it. He was over. He was gone. I said to my dad, you go. My father is a little-- was older. He's older. He was 43 years old. He went up and went to the second notch, and he slipped. And underneath was a big metal cover for something, a hole or something, and it made a big boom like a gong, you know. And I said, oh my God, now the Germans will come. And he went back in the toilet. It was quiet. He went up again. And I pushed him. He made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: He made it over the wall, and I went out to the toilet. There was a guard. I came out to the wall. I wanted to do it. There was already a, a German soldier. 'What are you doing here? In.' And he pushed me back into the barracks. And they closed. And this was it. We could not get out from the barracks anymore. The next morning, at 6 o'clock, we all had to get up, take our belongings, what we had.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody got four rolls, little rolls, and they put us in the trucks. It was maybe-- I would say about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 800 to 1,100 people-- Italian Jews. We were just a group of 30 Jews escaped from France, but they took all Italian Jews also. And we came to the train station, and there I saw again this family, this Jewish family with this girl. And I, I stick to them together, because my father was gone. Nobody-- my friend was gone. I was left alone. And so I hang onto this family to be with somebody. And we were loaded into the train and-- not train. Wagons. It was for, for animals, animals. So we were-- it says nine horses or 40 soldiers. And they put us in. We were maybe 60. And I was with this family. And they closed the door. And on the train, on the wagon of the train, was written Auschwitz. I did not know what is Auschwitz. And some people, they're, 'Oh, Auschwitz. I heard about it. We heard about it.' But they loaded us in. They closed the wagons. And we were nine days on the-- eight-- we were sent away November 5th. November 5th, we were deported. And I arrived in Auschwitz November 13.
